abstract: This research investigates the proficiency of large language models (LLMs) in representing geometric data and their associated spatial relationships. Utilizing models such as GPT-2 and BERT, we encode geometries provided in Well-Known Text (WKT) format and process the resulting embeddings through downstream classifiers and regressors. This framework allows us to evaluate how effectively LLM-generated representations capture fundamental geometric attributes. Our experimental results indicate that while these embeddings successfully preserve geometry types and identify certain spatial relations—achieving up to 73% accuracy—significant limitations persist in estimating precise numeric values and retrieving spatially related objects. These findings underscore a critical gap in the ability of current foundation models to grasp the nuances of geospatial data. We conclude that future GeoAI applications require more robust integration of domain-specific knowledge to better handle the complexities inherent in spatial reasoning.
